topics.title: Data Types & Variables
topics.description: Understand Python's core data types: int, float, str, bool.

lessons.title: What are data types?
lessons.body: Every value in Python has a type. The type determines what operations you can do with it and how it's stored in memory. Python figures out the type automatically — you don't need to declare it.

lessons.title: Integers and floats
lessons.body: Integers (int) are whole numbers like 1, -5, 100. Floats have decimal points like 3.14, -0.5. Dividing two integers with / always gives a float in Python 3.
lessons.code:
age = 25
pi = 3.14159
print(type(age))  # <class 'int'>
print(type(pi))   # <class 'float'>

lessons.title: Strings
lessons.body: Strings store text. Use single or double quotes — both work. You can even use triple quotes for multi-line strings.
lessons.code:
name = "Alice"
greeting = 'Hello'
print(name[0])  # 'A'

lessons.title: Booleans
lessons.body: Booleans are either True or False. They're the result of comparisons and logical operations.
lessons.code:
is_raining = True
print(5 > 3)       # True
print(True + True) # 2

topics.title: Printing & Output
topics.description: Learn how to display text and values on the screen using Python's print() function.

lessons.title: The print() function
lessons.body: print() is the simplest way to show output. You can pass any value or variable to it and Python will display it in the console.
lessons.code:
print("Hello, world!")
print(42)
print(3.14)

lessons.title: Printing multiple values
lessons.body: You can pass several values separated by commas. By default, print() adds a space between them and a newline at the end.
lessons.code:
name = "Alice"
age = 30
print("Name:", name, "Age:", age)
# Name: Alice Age: 30

lessons.title: f-strings
lessons.body: f-strings (formatted string literals) let you embed expressions directly inside a string. Prefix the string with f and place expressions in curly braces.
lessons.code:
name = "Bob"
score = 95
print(f"{name} scored {score} points.")
# Bob scored 95 points.

lessons.title: sep and end parameters
lessons.body: Use sep to change the separator between values, and end to change what's printed after the last value (default is a newline).
lessons.code:
print("a", "b", "c", sep="-")   # a-b-c
print("loading", end="...")
print("done")                    # loading...done

topics.title: Comments & Code Readability
topics.description: Discover how to write comments in Python to make your code easier to understand.

lessons.title: What is a comment?
lessons.body: A comment is a note in your code that Python ignores when running the program. Comments help you and others understand what the code does.

lessons.title: Single-line comments
lessons.body: Use the # character to start a comment. Everything after # on that line is ignored by Python.
lessons.code:
# This is a comment
x = 10  # This sets x to 10
print(x)

lessons.title: Multi-line comments
lessons.body: Python doesn't have a special multi-line comment syntax, but you can use triple-quoted strings as an informal block comment when they aren't assigned to a variable.
lessons.code:
"""
This block describes what the program does.
It spans multiple lines.
"""
print("Program starts here")

lessons.title: When to write comments
lessons.body: Write comments to explain why you made a decision, not just what the code does. Clear variable names often reduce the need for comments — but complex logic always benefits from an explanation.

topics.title: User Input
topics.description: Learn how to read text entered by the user at runtime using the input() function.

lessons.title: The input() function
lessons.body: input() pauses the program and waits for the user to type something and press Enter. It always returns a string.
lessons.code:
name = input("What is your name? ")
print("Hello,", name)

lessons.title: Converting input to a number
lessons.body: Because input() returns a string, you must convert it before doing arithmetic. Use int() for whole numbers or float() for decimals.
lessons.code:
age = int(input("Enter your age: "))
print("In 10 years you will be", age + 10)

lessons.title: Handling invalid input
lessons.body: If the user types something that can't be converted, Python raises a ValueError. You'll learn to handle errors properly later — for now, trust that the user enters valid data.
lessons.code:
number = float(input("Enter a decimal: "))
print("Doubled:", number * 2)

topics.title: Arithmetic Operators
topics.description: Explore Python's built-in arithmetic operators for performing calculations.

lessons.title: Basic operations
lessons.body: Python supports the four classic operations: addition (+), subtraction (-), multiplication (*), and division (/). Division always produces a float.

lessons.title: Floor division and modulo
lessons.body: // performs floor division — it divides and rounds down to the nearest integer. % gives the remainder after division.

lessons.title: Exponentiation
lessons.body: Use ** to raise a number to a power. It works with integers and floats.

lessons.title: Operator precedence
lessons.body: Python follows standard math rules: exponentiation first, then multiplication/division/modulo, then addition/subtraction. Use parentheses to control evaluation order.

topics.title: Comparison & Logical Operators
topics.description: Use comparison and logical operators to evaluate conditions that return True or False.

lessons.title: Comparison operators
lessons.body: Comparison operators compare two values and return a boolean. The six operators are: == (equal), != (not equal), >, <, >=, <=.
lessons.code:
print(5 == 5)   # True
print(5 != 3)   # True
print(10 > 7)   # True
print(4 >= 4)   # True

lessons.title: Logical and / or / not
lessons.body: and returns True if both conditions are True. or returns True if at least one is True. not flips a boolean.
lessons.code:
age = 20
has_id = True
print(age >= 18 and has_id)  # True
print(age < 18 or has_id)    # True
print(not has_id)             # False

lessons.title: Chained comparisons
lessons.body: Python lets you chain comparisons naturally, like in mathematics. This is more readable than using and explicitly.
lessons.code:
x = 5
print(1 < x < 10)   # True
print(0 <= x <= 5)  # True

topics.title: Type Conversion
topics.description: Learn how to convert values between Python's built-in types using constructor functions.

lessons.title: Why convert types?
lessons.body: Sometimes a value is stored as the wrong type. For example, input() always returns a string, but you may need a number. Python's built-in functions let you convert between types.

lessons.title: int(), float(), str()
lessons.body: int() converts to an integer, float() to a floating-point number, and str() to a string. Conversion can fail if the value isn't compatible.
lessons.code:
print(int("42"))     # 42
print(float("3.14")) # 3.14
print(str(100))      # '100'

lessons.title: bool() and truthiness
lessons.body: bool() converts a value to True or False. Most values are truthy; exceptions include 0, 0.0, empty strings, and None.
lessons.code:
print(bool(0))     # False
print(bool(""))    # False
print(bool(42))    # True
print(bool("hi"))  # True

lessons.title: Implicit vs explicit conversion
lessons.body: Python sometimes converts types automatically (implicit), such as promoting an int to float in mixed arithmetic. Explicit conversion is when you call int(), float(), etc. yourself — always prefer explicit to avoid surprises.
lessons.code:
print(3 + 1.5)      # 4.5 (int promoted to float)
print(int(3 + 1.5)) # 4   (explicit truncation)

topics.title: Conditionals: if / elif / else
topics.description: Control the flow of your program by executing different code based on conditions.

lessons.title: The if statement
lessons.body: An if statement runs a block of code only when a condition is True. The block must be indented (4 spaces by convention).

lessons.title: else and elif
lessons.body: else runs when the if condition is False. elif (else-if) lets you check additional conditions in sequence. Only the first matching branch executes.

lessons.title: Nested conditions
lessons.body: You can place if statements inside other if statements to check multiple related conditions. Keep nesting shallow to maintain readability.

lessons.title: One-line conditionals
lessons.body: Python supports a ternary expression: value_if_true if condition else value_if_false. Use it for simple, short choices.
lessons.code:
age = 20
status = "adult" if age >= 18 else "minor"
print(status)  # adult

topics.title: For Loops
topics.description: Repeat actions over sequences of values using Python's for loop.

lessons.title: Iterating over a sequence
lessons.body: A for loop goes through each item in a sequence (like a list or string) one at a time. The loop variable takes the value of each item in turn.

lessons.title: Using range()
lessons.body: range(n) produces integers from 0 up to (but not including) n. range(start, stop, step) gives you more control.

lessons.title: Looping over strings
lessons.body: Strings are sequences too. A for loop visits each character one by one.

lessons.title: break and continue
lessons.body: break exits the loop immediately. continue skips the rest of the current iteration and moves to the next one.

topics.title: While Loops
topics.description: Repeat a block of code as long as a condition remains True using the while loop.

lessons.title: Basic while loop
lessons.body: A while loop checks a condition before every iteration. If the condition is True, it runs the body; otherwise it stops.
lessons.code:
count = 0
while count < 5:
    print(count)
    count += 1

lessons.title: Infinite loops and break
lessons.body: If the condition never becomes False, the loop runs forever (an infinite loop). Use break to exit when a certain event occurs.

lessons.title: while vs for
lessons.body: Use a for loop when you know in advance how many times to iterate (or you're iterating over a collection). Use while when the number of iterations depends on a condition that changes during the loop.

topics.title: Defining Functions
topics.description: Package reusable logic into functions using def, parameters, and return values.

lessons.title: What is a function?
lessons.body: A function is a named block of code that performs a specific task. You define it once and can call it as many times as you need, avoiding repetition.

lessons.title: def, parameters, and return
lessons.body: Use def to define a function. Parameters are the inputs; return sends a value back to the caller.
lessons.code:
def add(a, b):
    return a + b

result = add(3, 4)
print(result)  # 7

lessons.title: Default parameter values
lessons.body: You can give parameters default values. If the caller doesn't supply an argument, the default is used instead.
lessons.code:
def greet(name, greeting="Hello"):
    return f"{greeting}, {name}!"

print(greet("Alice"))          # Hello, Alice!
print(greet("Bob", "Hi"))      # Hi, Bob!

lessons.title: Functions without a return value
lessons.body: A function that doesn't explicitly return a value implicitly returns None. These functions are useful for side effects like printing.
lessons.code:
def say_hello(name):
    print(f"Hello, {name}!")

result = say_hello("Charlie")
print(result)  # None

topics.title: Introduction to Lists
topics.description: Store multiple values in a single variable using Python lists.

lessons.title: Creating a list
lessons.body: A list is an ordered collection of items enclosed in square brackets. Items can be of any type, and a list can mix types.
lessons.code:
numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
names = ["Alice", "Bob"]
mixed = [1, "hello", True]
print(numbers)

lessons.title: Accessing items by index
lessons.body: Each item has an index starting at 0. Negative indices count from the end: -1 is the last item.
lessons.code:
colors = ["red", "green", "blue"]
print(colors[0])   # red
print(colors[-1])  # blue

lessons.title: Adding and removing items
lessons.body: append() adds an item to the end. remove() deletes the first occurrence of a value.
lessons.code:
items = ["a", "b", "c"]
items.append("d")
print(items)         # ['a', 'b', 'c', 'd']
items.remove("b")
print(items)         # ['a', 'c', 'd']

lessons.title: Useful list functions
lessons.body: len() returns the number of items. sorted() returns a new sorted list. sum() adds up numeric items.
lessons.code:
nums = [3, 1, 4, 1, 5]
print(len(nums))       # 5
print(sorted(nums))    # [1, 1, 3, 4, 5]
print(sum(nums))       # 14

topics.title: Indexing & Slicing
topics.description: Access specific elements or sub-sequences from strings and lists using indexing and slicing.

lessons.title: Zero-based indexing
lessons.body: Python counts positions from 0. The first element is at index 0, the second at 1, and so on. Negative indices count from the end.
lessons.code:
word = "Python"
print(word[0])   # P
print(word[-1])  # n

lessons.title: Slicing basics
lessons.body: A slice [start:stop] returns a portion of a sequence. start is inclusive, stop is exclusive. Omitting either defaults to the beginning or end.
lessons.code:
s = "Hello, World!"
print(s[0:5])   # Hello
print(s[7:])    # World!
print(s[:5])    # Hello

lessons.title: Step in slices
lessons.body: Adding a step [start:stop:step] skips elements. A step of -1 reverses the sequence.

topics.title: String Methods
topics.description: Manipulate and inspect text using Python's built-in string methods.

lessons.title: Case methods
lessons.body: upper() and lower() change the case of all characters. capitalize() uppercases only the first character.
lessons.code:
s = "hello WORLD"
print(s.upper())      # HELLO WORLD
print(s.lower())      # hello world
print(s.capitalize()) # Hello world

lessons.title: strip, split, join
lessons.body: strip() removes leading and trailing whitespace. split() breaks a string into a list. join() does the reverse — it combines a list of strings into one.
lessons.code:
text = "  hello  "
print(text.strip())              # 'hello'
words = "a,b,c".split(",")      # ['a', 'b', 'c']
print("-".join(words))           # a-b-c

lessons.title: Searching inside strings
lessons.body: find() returns the index of the first occurrence (-1 if not found). in is often simpler for a True/False check.
lessons.code:
msg = "Good morning!"
print(msg.find("morning"))  # 5
print("Good" in msg)        # True

lessons.title: replace()
lessons.body: replace(old, new) returns a new string with all occurrences of old replaced by new. Strings are immutable — replace() doesn't change the original.
lessons.code:
s = "I like cats and cats like me"
print(s.replace("cats", "dogs"))
# I like dogs and dogs like me

topics.title: None & the Absence of a Value
topics.description: Understand what None means in Python and how to check for it correctly.

lessons.title: What is None?
lessons.body: None is Python's way of representing 'nothing' or 'no value'. It is its own type (NoneType) and is the only value of that type.
lessons.code:
x = None
print(x)          # None
print(type(x))    # <class 'NoneType'>

lessons.title: Functions that return None
lessons.body: Functions that don't explicitly return a value return None automatically. Forgetting to return is a common source of bugs.
lessons.code:
def double(n):
    result = n * 2  # forgot return!

val = double(5)
print(val)  # None

lessons.title: Checking for None
lessons.body: Always use is None or is not None to check for None, not == None. The is operator checks identity, which is the correct semantics for singleton objects like None.

topics.title: Lists & List Methods
topics.description: Learn how to store, access, and manipulate ordered collections of items using Python lists.

lessons.title: Creating a list
lessons.body: A list is an ordered, mutable collection. Create one with square brackets.
lessons.code:
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]
print(fruits[0])  # 'apple'

lessons.title: Common methods
lessons.body: append() adds to the end. remove() deletes the first matching value. pop() removes by index and returns the value.
lessons.code:
fruits.append("date")
fruits.remove("banana")
last = fruits.pop()

lessons.title: Slicing
lessons.body: Use [start:stop:step] to get a sub-list. Negative indices count from the end.

lessons.title: List comprehensions
lessons.body: A concise way to create a new list by transforming or filtering another sequence.
lessons.code:
squares = [x**2 for x in range(1, 6)]
# [1, 4, 9, 16, 25]

topics.title: Decorators
topics.description: Understand how Python decorators wrap functions to add behaviour without modifying the original code.

lessons.title: Functions as first-class objects
lessons.body: In Python, functions are objects. You can pass them as arguments, return them, and assign them to variables.
lessons.code:
def greet():
    return "Hello"

say = greet
print(say())  # 'Hello'

lessons.title: Writing a decorator
lessons.body: A decorator is a function that takes another function and returns a new function with added behaviour.

lessons.title: Applying with @
lessons.body: The @ syntax is syntactic sugar for reassigning the function through the decorator.

lessons.title: functools.wraps
lessons.body: Use @functools.wraps(fn) inside your wrapper to preserve the original function's name and docstring.

topics.title: AsyncIO & Coroutines
topics.description: Master Python's asyncio framework for writing concurrent I/O-bound code using coroutines.

lessons.title: The event loop
lessons.body: asyncio runs an event loop that schedules coroutines. The loop runs until all coroutines complete. asyncio.run() is the entry point in modern Python.

lessons.title: async def and await
lessons.body: Coroutines are defined with async def. Use await to pause execution until an awaitable completes.
lessons.code:
import asyncio

async def fetch(url: str) -> str:
    await asyncio.sleep(1)  # simulates I/O
    return f"data from {url}"

lessons.title: Running concurrently with gather
lessons.body: asyncio.gather() runs multiple coroutines concurrently. They interleave during I/O waits.
lessons.code:
async def main():
    results = await asyncio.gather(
        fetch("https://a.example"),
        fetch("https://b.example"),
    )
    print(results)

asyncio.run(main())

lessons.title: Tasks and cancellation
lessons.body: asyncio.create_task() schedules a coroutine immediately. You can cancel a task at any time.
lessons.code:
task = asyncio.create_task(fetch("https://c.example"))
await asyncio.sleep(0.5)
task.cancel()
